title: Deep Time
whatItIs:
Deep time is the study of time at geological, evolutionary, and planetary scales. It makes ordinary human time feel small, but in a useful way.

The lens helps you compare the pace of rocks, species, climates, and civilisations without confusing them.
whyItMatters:
It matters because many things we treat as fixed are actually recent and temporary. Climate, species, coastlines, and even human institutions change when viewed over long enough horizons.

Deep time also builds humility. It makes clear how brief a human lifespan is compared with the systems we depend on.
keyIdeas: Human time is short relative to planetary time
Long timescales change what counts as normal
Evolution and geology are both slow and decisive
frontier: The frontier is how to think about planetary futures without losing sight of near-term action. Climate, biodiversity, and civilisation all sit inside much longer arcs than politics usually does.
openQuestions: How should long time horizons change planning?
What can humans realistically influence at deep-time scale?
How much of today's world will matter in ten thousand years?
